main_ingredients: rice paper or spring roll wrapper

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 3 pieces (approximately 90 g), as described in the HPB Singapore food composition entry for this dish.

nutrient_source.title: Unagi Cheese Roll — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Unagi Cheese Roll (G00010692). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are sourced from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on spring roll / fried wrapper with mixed protein-fat filling
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Unagi Cheese Roll. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the closest analogous food: fried spring roll / dim sum wrapper with protein-fat filling, which attenuates glycaemic response relative to plain refined carbohydrate. Carbohydrate per serving = 7.97 g (8.85 g × 0.90 kg serving). GL = (55 × 7.97) / 100 = 4.4, rounded to 4.3. Source: University of Sydney GI Database analogous category; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1279 mg/100 g — more than double the 600 mg/100 g high-concern threshold
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Unagi Cheese Roll; a single 90 g serving delivers approximately 1151 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the 2000 mg daily limit, and the combination of high sodium and estimated saturated fat (~9.5 g/100 g) from processed cheese further elevates cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 6.24 g/100 g from unagi glaze (mirin and sugar) combined with zero dietary fibre, producing a moderate glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion to 1–2 pieces and pair with high-fibre vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response; the absence of dietary fibre and the sweet unagi glaze elevate glycaemic impact despite the low GI of the fried wrapper.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~9.5 g/100 g from processed cheese and sodium 1279 mg/100 g, both exceeding cardiovascular risk thresholds
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of Unagi Cheese Roll; the high saturated fat from processed cheese raises LDL cholesterol, the extreme sodium load promotes arterial stiffness and fluid retention, and cholesterol at 78 mg/100 g adds further atherogenic burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~9.5 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold, promoting pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); sodium 1279 mg/100 g may exacerbate joint swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise consumption; processed cheese is the primary saturated fat driver and should be reduced or substituted, while the high sodium content may worsen synovial inflammation and tissue oedema.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Unagi Cheese Roll is a Singaporean Chinese fusion dish consisting of grilled freshwater eel (unagi) and creamy processed cheese wrapped in a thin pastry or spring roll skin and deep-fried until golden and crisp, typically served in portions of 3 pieces. The dish draws on Japanese unagi preparation traditions — where eel is glazed with a sweet soy-mirin-sugar tare sauce — and combines it with the richness of melted processed cheese, creating a contrast of savoury, sweet, and umami flavours with a crispy exterior and soft, creamy interior. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at 307 kcal/100 g, with high fat (23.06 g/100 g, estimated saturated fat ~9.5 g/100 g from processed cheese), moderate protein (16.09 g/100 g from eel and cheese), and low carbohydrate (8.85 g/100 g). Sodium is extremely high at 1279 mg/100 g, driven by processed cheese, unagi sauce, and nori, making this dish a significant concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. Calcium is notably high at 1375 mg/100 g, reflecting the processed cheese content. Dietary fibre is absent (0 g/100 g). The dish is not suitable for patients with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis, and requires careful portion control for patients with Type 2 Diabetes. It is commonly served as a starter or side dish at Singaporean Chinese seafood restaurants and zi char stalls.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010692) with a stated confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from the known processed cheese content as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this single estimation reduces certainty for that sub-field only. GI is an analogous estimate. Overall record confidence is high given the canonical HPB provenance for all primary nutrient fields.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (9.5 g/100 g) is estimated from the known processed cheese component of this dish; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. This estimate is consistent with typical processed cheese saturated fat content (~30–35% of total fat) applied to the reported total fat of 23.06 g/100 g. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, fat-soluble vitamins, B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific composite dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per the HPB source record; this reflects the absence of fibre-containing ingredients in the fried wrapper and processed cheese filling. 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on fried spring roll / dim sum wrapper with protein-fat filling from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Unagi Cheese Roll exists in any approved source. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; unagi (eel) is considered non-halal by some Islamic jurisprudence schools, and processed cheese may contain non-halal rennet. 6. Cuisine is classified as Singaporean Chinese (fusion) reflecting the local zi char context despite the Japanese-origin ingredient (unagi).
